turns.content: I'm new to this project and need to understand how we parse binary objects. Is it just a simple string split?

That's not quite right; parsing object files requires understanding the specific format like ELF or Mach-O, which defines where sections start and how symbols are indexed. You cannot treat them as plain text strings because they contain structural data that must be interpreted according to their header layout.
I see. So we load the whole file into memory first?
Exactly, you read the entire binary into a Vec<u8> or similar buffer type so that your parser can walk through the headers and section tables sequentially without needing random access from disk.
Got it. But what if we don't load everything at once? Can we use something like an iterator over bytes?

You could, but for most object parsers, loading the full buffer first is simpler because you need to know where each section begins relative to others. The crate usually provides traits like IntoIterator or specific Reader implementations that wrap your Vec<u8> so you can iterate through the file structure safely.
That makes sense. But what about symbols? Are they just a list of strings?

No, symbols in object files are structured entries with names, types, and binding attributes that follow the format specification. You must parse each symbol's offset and size correctly to understand how it relates to other parts of the binary.
Right. So when I write my own parser, do I just assume every file is ELF?

No, you must check the magic number at the start of the buffer to determine if it's ELF, Mach-O, or COFF, then switch your parsing logic accordingly. The crate likely has a trait like ParseObject that handles this dispatch internally so you don't have to write separate parsers for each format manually.

I'm trying to understand why Rust crates for binary parsing exist and how they handle the raw bytes, but it feels like I'm just dumping data into a format that doesn't match my mental model of what an object actually is. Why does this even work this way?

It's totally understandable to feel confused when you first encounter something like the ELF or Mach-O formats because they look like magic numbers compared to how we usually structure code in Rust. The key insight here is that these crates are essentially acting as translators between those binary layouts and high-level Rust types, so instead of manually mapping every byte offset, you can just let them handle the parsing for you using traits like IntoIterator or specific deserialization functions tailored to the format.
So I should be reading raw bytes directly without any intermediate steps? That sounds inefficient compared to what I've seen in other languages where everything is pre-processed.

Actually, no one is really doing that anymore because it's just too error-prone and slow. Modern Rust crates like obj or elf-reader do the heavy lifting by using safe abstractions so you don't have to deal with manual memory management or offset calculations yourself. They wrap those raw bytes into structured types like ObjectFile or SectionInfo, which makes your code much cleaner.
But wait, I thought Rust was all about zero-copy operations and avoiding allocations, but these crates seem to be allocating a lot of intermediate structures for every symbol read?

You're right that the goal is efficiency, but those crates are doing something clever by using smart allocation strategies like pooling or lazy initialization so you only allocate when you actually need specific sections rather than loading everything upfront. Instead of making your program crash with a segfault from bad pointers, they use safe abstractions to guarantee correctness while still keeping performance high.

I get it now about the safety, but I'm still stuck on how these crates handle the different architectures like x86_64 versus ARM without me having to manually check every single byte pattern. It feels like there's too much hidden complexity here.
Wait, does that mean I can't write my own parser if I want more control? Or is it just better to use these crates?

You absolutely can write your own parser if you really need granular control over every detail of the binary layout, but for most applications using established crates like obj or elf-reader is way safer and faster because they've already solved all those edge cases around different architectures and endianness issues. However, if you do want to implement something custom, Rust makes it easy by letting you define your own traits and derive implementations.
Okay so the crates are basically just providing a standard interface that everyone uses? That's why they're so popular then?

Exactly! The community has converged on these specific crates because they provide consistent interfaces for parsing ELF, COFF, and Mach-O files without needing to worry about low-level details. Instead of reinventing the wheel every time you want to read a symbol table or section header, you just import the crate and call their methods.
But what if I need to parse something that isn't one of these standard formats? Can I still use Rust for that?

You can absolutely! If you're dealing with a custom binary format or a newer architecture like RISC-V, you don't have to wait for a new crate. You can just write your own parser in Rust using the same pattern of wrapping raw bytes into safe types and implementing traits for iteration or deserialization.
So I should stop worrying about whether my code is idiomatic and just focus on writing parsers that are correct first?

That's a great mindset shift! While correctness is the foundation, idiomatic Rust also means using standard crates when available because they follow community conventions. Instead of reinventing something proven to work well, you can leverage existing tools like obj or elf-reader which handle complex cases automatically so your code stays readable and maintainable.

One last thing I'm confused about is how these crates deal with the different symbol types across platforms without me having to manually parse each one separately. It feels like there's magic happening in the background.

You're noticing exactly what makes Rust great here! Those crates use polymorphic traits and generic type parameters so they can handle symbols from any platform uniformly under a single interface. Instead of writing separate parsing logic for every architecture, you just implement one trait once and it works everywhere because the crate handles the underlying format differences internally.
